meta.title: Wat Phnom Daun Penh — Phnom Penh Travel Guide
meta.description: Explore Wat Phnom Daun Penh, one of Phnom Penh's most iconic Buddhist landmarks. Discover its history, Khmer architecture, visitor tips and how to get there.

hero.localName: វត្តភ្នំដូនពេញ
hero.pronounce: វត្តភ្នំដូនពេញ · /vɔət pɲom/
hero.title: Wat Phnom Daun Penh
hero.subtitle: One of Phnom Penh's most iconic historic Buddhist landmarks, perched on a hill above the city center.
hero.legend: A sacred tree, five statues, the birth of a city. In 1372 the wealthy widow Lady Penh (Daun Penh) found a drifting Koki tree on the Tonlé Sap riverbank; within it lay four bronze Buddhas and one stone Vishnu. She led her neighbors to mound the earth into a hill and enshrine the relics — and Phnom Penh ('the hill of Penh') was founded, with Wat Phnom as the spiritual heart of the millennium-old capital.
hero.heroAlt: Wat Phnom Daun Penh temple on a hill in central Phnom Penh, Cambodia

liveStatus.label: Current status
liveStatus.openHint: Visitors welcome — last entry before close
liveStatus.closedHint: Opens daily; plan your visit for 07:00–19:00

pocketGuide.note: A print-ready one-page summary — save as PDF or print and carry it with you.

audioGuide.legendText: Hear the 14th-century story of how a drifting Koki tree gave the city its name.
audioGuide.architectureText: A 30-second walkthrough of murals, the naga stair and the great stupa.

intro.title: About Wat Phnom
intro.description: Wat Phnom Daun Penh is one of Phnom Penh's most famous historic temples, sitting atop a small hill in the city center. It is both an active Buddhist site and a cultural landmark tied to the very origin of the city's name.

highlights.title: Why it's worth a visit
items: The birthplace of the legend behind Phnom Penh's name
A living Buddhist temple still busy with devotees
Khmer art, murals and sacred relics

guide.title: Plan your visit
guide.description: The hilltop is compact and easy to combine with other central Phnom Penh sights.
items: Allow 30–60 minutes for a relaxed visit
Dress modestly when entering the temple
Pair it with the riverfront and Royal Palace area
Go early for cooler weather and fewer crowds

history.title: The history of Wat Phnom
history.subtitle: From a 14th-century legend to the spiritual heart of Phnom Penh.

sections.heading: The legend of Lady Penh
sections.body: A wealthy widow, Lady Penh (Daun Penh, 'Grandmother Penh'), found four bronze Buddha statues — and one stone Vishnu — hidden inside a drifting Koki tree on the riverbank. She summoned her neighbors to mound earth into a hill (phnom) and enshrine the relics in a wooden shrine. The act gave the city its name: Phnom Penh, 'the hill of Penh'.

sections.heading: Capital moves to Phnom Penh
sections.body: King Ponhea Yat relocated the Khmer capital from Angkor to Phnom Penh. He rebuilt Wat Phnom and raised a great white stupa (chedi) on the summit to house his ashes. The humble hill shrine was elevated into a royal spiritual center.

sections.heading: A growing sacred hill
sections.body: Across centuries the shrine was repeatedly rebuilt and enlarged. The artificial hill became the focus of local faith, and the surrounding settlement gradually grew into today's capital.

sections.heading: French Protectorate care
sections.body: Under the French Protectorate, successive kings restored and expanded the temple. The stupa and vihear (prayer hall) took on the later Khmer restoration style still seen today.

sections.heading: Hébrard's restoration
sections.body: The renowned architect Ernest Hébrard led a major restoration during the colonial period, shaping the classic silhouette of Wat Phnom that visitors recognize today.

sections.heading: Symbol of the capital
sections.body: Today Wat Phnom stands as a national cultural emblem and the namesake hill of Phnom Penh, welcoming devotees and travelers from around the world.

history.legendTitle: The founding legend of Lady Penh
history.legendText: The tale of Lady Penh and the drifting Koki tree is Phnom Penh's best-loved origin story. Inside the tree she found four bronze Buddhas and a stone Vishnu — a quiet sign of how Cambodian Buddhism blended with earlier Hindu devotion. The mound she raised became the hill, and her shrine became the temple that still gives the capital its name.

architecture.title: Khmer Buddhist architecture
architecture.subtitle: A layered hilltop sanctuary: stupa, shrines, murals and sacred trees.

features.title: Central stupa
features.text: The tall white stupa enshrines sacred relics and marks the spiritual center of the hill.

features.title: Buddha images
features.text: Revered Buddha statues — including those tied to the founding legend — are enshrined within the vihear.

features.title: Vihear murals
features.text: Painted in the early 20th century, the hall murals depict the Buddha's Jataka tales woven with scenes from the Khmer epic Reamker (Ramayana). Look up: the ceiling shows a cosmic mandala and the pillars are sheathed in gold.

features.title: Khmer ornament
features.text: Naga balustrades, lotus motifs and carved lintels showcase classic Khmer craftsmanship.

features.title: Seven-headed Naga stair
features.text: The dramatic stairway guarded by a seven-headed naga (serpent) symbolises the rainbow bridge linking the human world to the cosmos (Mount Meru) — a stairway to the heavens.

features.title: Sacred tree & shrines
features.text: Shaded trees and Lady Penh's small votive shrine dot the hilltop, lending a calm, contemplative atmosphere.

features.title: Hilltop layout
features.text: The man-made hill lifts the temple above the streets, offering a quiet overlook of the city.

features.title: Preah Chau Shrine
features.text: On the hill's north side stands the Chinese sanctuary (Preah Chau), where Khmer, Chinese and Vietnamese folk faiths meet. Merchants and students come to pray for wealth, success in studies, or draw a fortune stick (kien).

gallery.title: Moments at Wat Phnom
gallery.subtitle: A visual walk through the temple, its art and the surrounding city.
gallery.viewMaps: View Wat Phnom on Google Maps

experience.title: What visitors can do
experience.subtitle: A short but fulfilling stop for culture, calm and city views.

items.title: Visit the temple
items.text: Observe local devotion in the vihear and learn about daily Buddhist practice.

items.title: Enjoy the city view
items.text: Overlook central Phnom Penh from the hilltop.

items.title: Photograph the details
items.text: Great for architectural and cultural photography — stupa, murals and ornament.

items.title: Feel local life
items.text: Watch residents come to pray, make offerings and seek blessings.

bestTime.title: When to visit Wat Phnom
bestTime.subtitle: The hilltop is open daily; each part of the day has its own mood.

day.label: Morning
day.text: Cooler and quieter — a peaceful time for photography and prayer.

day.label: Afternoon
day.text: Warm, directional light — good for capturing architecture and murals.

day.label: Evening
day.text: As the lights come on, the hilltop feels especially atmospheric at day's end.

bestTime.note: Busiest mid-morning and on weekends; go early for a quieter experience.

transport.title: How to reach Wat Phnom
transport.subtitle: Wat Phnom sits in central Phnom Penh, easy to reach from most parts of the city.
transport.overview: The hill is near the junction of Norodom Boulevard and Street 96, a short ride from the riverfront and the Royal Palace.

modes.title: Tuk Tuk
modes.description: The most common way to get around Phnom Penh.
details: Fares vary by distance and time

modes.title: Taxi / Ride app
modes.description: Good for families and hot weather.
details: Metered taxis and ride apps available

modes.title: On foot
modes.description: Walkable from the central riverfront area.

modes.title: From the airport
modes.description: Phnom Penh International Airport (PNH) to Wat Phnom.
details: About 10 km / 25–40 min by taxi or ride app
Fixed-price airport taxi at the rank
Tuk tuk also possible in good weather

map.title: Find Wat Phnom on the map
map.subtitle: Central Phnom Penh, on the hill at the end of Wat Phnom Street.
map.addressValue: Wat Phnom Road, Phnom Penh, Cambodia

nearby.title: Explore around Wat Phnom
nearby.subtitle: Easy regional highlights near the hill.

places.name: Royal Palace area
places.text: Home to the Royal Palace and Silver Pagoda — Phnom Penh's most important royal and cultural district.

places.name: Riverfront (Tonlé Sap & Mekong)
places.text: A breezy promenade at the confluence of two rivers, great for walking and city views.

places.name: Central Market (Phsar Thmei)
places.text: A striking Art Deco market showcasing local commerce and everyday Phnom Penh life.

food.title: Food culture near Wat Phnom
food.subtitle: Taste Cambodian cuisine — with cultural context, not restaurant picks.
food.intro: Classic Cambodian flavors can be found around the hill. Below is an introduction to locals' food culture, not a recommendation of specific vendors.

items.name: Khmer breakfast
items.text: Rice porridge (babor) and French bread — a morning blend of local and French-influenced tastes.

items.name: Cambodian curry
items.text: Kari sach moan — a mild, coconut-rich curry of chicken, potato and lemongrass, slow-cooked.

items.name: Grilled meats
items.text: Skewered and grilled meats sold at street stalls and local eateries.

items.name: Street snacks
items.text: Palm-sugar cookies (nom kong) and fried noodles are everyday favorites.

items.name: Iced Khmer coffee
items.text: Strong coffee with condensed milk — a daily ritual of Cambodian coffee culture.

practical.title: Travel essentials
practical.subtitle: Everything you need for a smooth, respectful visit.

cards.title: Opening hours
cards.text: Open daily 07:00–19:00; early morning is coolest and quietest.

cards.title: Entry
cards.text: Check on-site for the latest information and follow local customs.

cards.title: Suggested stay
cards.text: Allow about 30–60 minutes for the hilltop, temple and views.

cards.title: Good for
cards.text: Culture lovers, photographers and families exploring central Phnom Penh.

etiquette.title: Temple etiquette
etiquette.subtitle: A few simple customs make your visit respectful and welcome.

cards.title: Dress modestly
cards.text: Cover shoulders and knees — no sleeveless tops, shorts or short skirts.

cards.title: Shoes & hats
cards.text: Remove shoes and hats before entering the vihear (hall); never point your feet at the Buddha.

cards.title: Photo courtesy
cards.text: Ask before photographing monks; keep quiet and respectful inside the hall.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.subtitle: Quick answers to plan your Wat Phnom visit.

items.a: Yes — it is one of Phnom Penh's most important cultural landmarks and the namesake hill of the city.

items.q: How long should I spend at Wat Phnom?
items.a: Most visitors spend 30 minutes to 1 hour on the hilltop, temple and views.
Dress modestly covering shoulders and knees when visiting the temple.
In central Phnom Penh, Cambodia, on the hill at the end of Wat Phnom Street.

items.q: When is the best time to visit?
items.a: Early morning is cool and less crowded; evening light is warm with a strong city atmosphere.

footer.tagline: A free, multilingual cultural guide to Phnom Penh's historic hilltop temple.
footer.rights: This is an independent information guide, not affiliated with any temple, business or government body.
footer.missionText: We turn verified facts, history and practical visitor information into a free, friendly, multilingual guide — with no commercial bookings or recommendations.
footer.addressValue: Wat Phnom Road, Phnom Penh, Cambodia
footer.disclaimer: Images are illustrative. Confirm opening hours and entry on-site before you go.

amenities.title: Facilities & Nearby Services
amenities.subtitle: Neutral, non-commercial reference for planning your visit — no specific businesses recommended.

cards.title: Restrooms
cards.text: Public toilets at the southeast base of the hill and near the main stairway entrance. A small upkeep fee (≈500–1,000 riel) may apply — bring small change and tissues.

cards.title: Parking & Drop-off
cards.text: Tuk-tuk / ride-hail drop-off around the roundabout (Norodom Blvd × St 96). Motorbikes park in marked bays with an attendant; cars/buses use nearby commercial or underground parking, then walk 3–5 min.

cards.title: Dining & Refreshments
cards.text: Street vendors by the park path sell fresh coconut water, chilled bottled water and Khmer snacks. Cafés and air-conditioned restaurants line Norodom Blvd within 3–8 min walk.

cards.title: Accommodation Areas
cards.text: Grouped only by district for neutrality: Riverside (~800m–1.5km), Norodom Blvd / Central (~500m–2km), and Central Market area (~1–1.5km). No specific hotels named.

cards.title: Convenience & Supplies
cards.text: 24-hour convenience stores within 500m for sunscreen, tissues, mosquito repellent and water. Phsar Thmei (Central Market) is ~1.2km away for fruit and daily goods.

cards.title: Fuel & EV Charging
cards.text: Standard fuel stations line Monivong/Norodom Blvds. EV charging is concentrated in shopping-mall basements and some modern stations; nearest hub is a commercial plaza ~5–10 min by car.
